sections.title: What is a Venn Diagram?
points: A Venn Diagram uses circles to show how sets overlap.
Each region shows who belongs to one set, both sets, or neither.
It is a useful way to organise information visually.

sections.title: Golden Rule: Fill the Middle First
points: Always place the members that belong to both sets in the intersection first.
This makes it easier to fill the remaining 'only' regions.
Example: A red circle goes in the middle if it is red and a circle.

sections.title: Fill the Only Regions
points: After the middle, place objects that belong only to one set.
Left side is A only, right side is B only.
Example: A red square is red but not a circle, so it belongs in A only.

sections.title: Outside the Circles
points: Objects that do not belong to either set go outside the circles.
This region still stays inside the Universal Set.
Example: A blue square that is not red and not a circle belongs outside.

sections.text: Tip: If the question gives totals instead of lists, use subtraction: circle total minus intersection gives the 'only' region.

cards.fact: A diagram that shows set relationships using overlapping circles.
cards.mnemonic: Venn diagrams help you see overlap clearly.

cards.fact: The middle region where both sets overlap.
A region for members of one set but not the other.
Members that are not in either circle.
